summary: O'Neil examines two interconnected issues in finance: the costs and benefits of high-frequency trading, and the problematic relationship between finance firms and the open-source software community. She questions who truly benefits from HFT beyond the direct profiteers, and highlights the societal risks of opaque algorithmic systems. Drawing on her experience at D.E. Shaw, she describes how finance firms extensively use open-source software but resist contributing back, citing competitive concerns even for tools unrelated to trading strategies. While acknowledging that contributions eventually leak out through departing employees, she notes this process is frustratingly slow.
key_points: HFT primarily benefits those profiting directly from it, while the broader societal benefits (like lower trading fees) may be offset by predatory practices against 'dumb money' participants
The true cost of HFT includes systemic risk from poorly understood black boxes colliding with each other, plus the opportunity cost of talented people working on zero-sum games
Finance firms like Goldman Sachs and D.E. Shaw extensively use open-source software but actively resist contributing improvements back to the community
The excuse for not sharing is competitive fear, even when tools are completely unrelated to actual trading algorithms
Open-source contributions from finance eventually happen through departing employees, representing a 'lagged contribution'
money_quotes: A harder and more complete answer would involve how much risk we take on as a society when we build black boxes that we don't understand and let them collide with each other with our money.
After all, you say market making, I say arbing.
Goldman had a one-way relationship with open source. They took huge amounts of free software off the Web, but they did not return it after he had modified it.

The urge to give back to the OS community was largely thwarted, when it came up at all, because there was a fear, or at least an argument, that somehow our competition would use it against us, to eliminate our edge.
He's got a way with words and he's bloodthirsty, always an entertaining combination.
As a modeler, you have a very selfish outlook and only want to develop tools for your particular situation.
key_insight: Finance firms benefit enormously from the open-source commons while contributing almost nothing back, treating shared resources as competitive advantages to be hoarded rather than community goods to be nurtured.
